exemplar.title: An Investigation Into The Effect Of Color On Memory

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation Into The Effect Of Color On Memory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: kgf128_IA Psychology_AN INVESTIGATION INTO THE EFFECT OF COLOR ON MEMORY.pdf

strengths: Investigation aim clearly stated and relevance to educational practice explained
Accurate summary of Baddeley & Hitchâs model with explicit connection to colour cues
Hypotheses correctly operationalise IV and DV and are precisely worded
weaknesses: Theory connection could be deepened by elaborating how highlighting engages specific memory subsystems
Opening paragraphs contain run-on sentences that obscure meaning
Floating citations and lack of contextual critique limit depth of background
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (aim/relevance and theory description) sit solidly in band 5 and one (hypotheses/variables) in 6. Although the hypotheses are exemplary, minor issues in clarity and depth prevent a full top-band award. An overall score of 5 reflects strong framing but acknowledges stylistic and elaborative limitations.

strengths: Research design is not just described but explained in terms of efficiency and validity
Participant demographics and procedure are detailed, supporting reproducibility
Controlled variables and materials are comprehensively listed with some explanation
weaknesses: Explanation of sampling omits discussion of bias and impact on external validity
Rationale linking each controlled variable to threats to validity needs further depth
Minor typos and incomplete ethical data-handling details detract from clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design strand merits the top of the band (4), but sampling and variables/materials are only mid-band (3) due to limited depth in bias discussion and variable justification. Averaging these assessments yields an overall score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics with accurate calculations
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with correct graph type and labels
Interpretation links statistical findings to the hypothesis and data clearly
weaknesses: Graph lacks error bars and consistent numerical scales
Inconsistent formatting of p-values and decimal places
Statistical commentary is solid but could include deeper explanation of decision criteria
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fit the 5-6 band, with minor presentation and commentary errors keeping the work from the very top (6). A score of 5 reflects accurate application and linkage but acknowledges small formatting and elaboration issues.

strengths: Limitations are clearly stated and explained with relevance to validity and reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified
Findings discussion connects to background theory and model
weaknesses: The theoretical discussion is brief and lacks deeper critical engagement with why effects may be absent
Modifications are sound but not prioritised by impact
Broader implications and future directions are not fully synthesized
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strengths/limitations and modifications strands merit top-band scores (5 each) due to clear explanation and justified changes. Discussion of findings is only mid-band (3) because it remains descriptive. Balancing these leads to an overall score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph clearly contrasts mean correct answers for the two conditions and directly addresses the hypothesis. Axes and conditions are labelled; however, numerical scale and error bars are absent, so minor errors persist. This is consistent with the lower end of the top band.

Findings are interpreted in writing: the p-value is compared with the 0.05 level, the null hypothesis is accepted, and the small mean difference is commented on. The interpretation is explicitly linked to the hypothesis and data, fulfilling the 5-6 descriptor; limited elaboration justifies a 5.

Both IV and DV are not only identified but also operationalised: IV = type of text (yellow-highlighted vs plain black); DV = number of correct answers on a ten-item worksheet. Null and research hypotheses are correctly worded in terms of âsignificant differenceâ. This meets the top descriptor; the articulation is precise, warranting the upper score in the band.

Self-selected sampling is described and its motivational advantage is explained; random allocation to conditions is noted. While the explanation addresses strengths and weaknesses, it is fairly brief, so a mid-band mark of 3 is appropriate within the 3-4 band.

The student summarises Baddeley & Hitchâs Working Memory Model and links the visuospatial sketchpad and episodic buffer to the processing of colour cues that may aid transfer to long-term memory. Although brief, the theory description is accurate and the connection to the present investigation is explicitly made, fulfilling the 5-6 band; a score of 5 reflects that the link is competent but not deeply developed.

The student connects the nonsignificant findings to Huchendorf (2007) and to theoretical claims about colour and memory, but the discussion is brief and largely descriptive. Depth of critical engagement is limited, positioning the work in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

The aim â to investigate whether colour (yellow highlighting) influences memory for textual information â is explicitly stated and its practical relevance for educational practice is clearly explained (use of highlighters to improve study efficiency). This satisfies the descriptor for a full explanation of relevance, justifying placement in the 5-6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate because the rationale, while clear, is concise rather than richly elaborated.

Multiple strengths and limitations (design, sampling, standardisation, environment, reading time) are identified and explained in relation to validity and reliability, meeting the descriptor for the top band. Explanations are adequate but not exhaustive; hence a score of 5.

Several controlled variables (environment, time limits, standardised instructions) and materials (highlighted vs plain text, identical worksheet, timer) are listed and the reasoning for their selection is sketched, meeting the requirement for âexplainedâ. Limited depth keeps the score to 3 within the 3-4 band.

Each limitation discussed (small sample, sampling bias, short reading time, environmental variance) is followed by a concrete modification (increase sample size, adopt random sampling, extend reading time, standardise rooms) with clear justification. This satisfies the highest descriptor; breadth is good, depth moderate, warranting a 5.

The design (independent measures) is not only described but also explained with reference to time efficiency and reduced carry-over effects, matching the 3-4 descriptor. The explanation is coherent, so the top of the band (4) is awarded.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics are used and calculations appear accurate; the choice of test is justified with regard to distribution and independent groups. Minor wording issues exist but no calculation errors are evident, so the strand fits the 5-6 band; a 5 reflects strong but not exemplary statistical commentary.

annotations.comment: The Working Memory Model is accurately summarised and linked to colour cue processing, but the connection could be deepened by explaining how highlighting specifically engages the visuospatial sketchpad and episodic buffer.
Sentence structure in the opening paragraph is often run-on and lacks punctuation, which obscures meaning. Break complex sentences and clarify subjectâverb relationships.
The reflection form is a valuable ethical tool, but it would be helpful to specify how these reflections inform data analysis or participant well-being monitoring.
Highlighting the independent measures designâs minimisation of demand characteristics demonstrates strong internal validity consideration.
Proposed shift to random sampling directly addresses a stated limitation and will enhance external validityâthis linkage shows thoughtful evaluation.
Participant variables (age, language, academic level) are well controlled and random allocation is noted, demonstrating strong internal validity.
Several limitations (reading time, environment variance) are identified; prioritise modifications by impact to show strategic planning.
The summary of Huchendorfâs (2007) study is detailed and clear, but integrating a brief critique or context of its methodology would strengthen the rationale for your replication.
The opening citation â(Huchendorf, 2007).â is disconnected; consider integrating it into a sentence or providing context to avoid a floating reference.
The description of how participants are divided into coloured and control groups effectively sets up the experimental manipulationâthis provides a clear procedural link to the original study.
The investigation aim is clearly stated and linked to educational relevance, but you could operationally define what constitutes âmemory performanceâ in this aim section.
Both the null and research hypotheses are correctly phrased and operationalise IV (text highlighting) and DV (number of correct answers), demonstrating strong experimental framing.
The independent measures design is justified with efficiency and external validity benefits; however, potential downsides (individual differences, reduced statistical power) should also be discussed.
Self-selected sampling is described and motivational merits are explained, but the description omits discussion of resultant sampling bias and its impact on external validity.
Participant demographics are clearly specified, including age range and language proficiency, which supports internal validity by ensuring comprehension of the text.
Controlled variables are listed comprehensively, but the rationale linking each control (noise, instructions, timing) to specific threat to validity should be expanded.
The description of the coloured groupâs materials clearly explains why highlighting guides attention to key terms, strengthening construct validity.
The control groupâs material description is adequate but should justify how the plain text version matches cognitive load of the highlighted version.
Using the same worksheet for both groups is a solid standardisation tactic, enhancing fairness and reducing procedural bias.
The timer control is well justified, but consider discussing timer precision and how slight variances in timing might impact participant performance.
Step 2 repeats âcolored groupâ twice; correct this typo to avoid confusion about group sizes.
The procedural list is detailed and clear, which aids reproducibilityâthis level of clarity strengthens methodological rigour.
Ethical considerations are well addressed, but clarify how anonymised reflection data will be analysed or stored securely to complete the ethical protocol.
The decision to use means and standard deviations appropriately summarises participantsâ performance by condition.
Table 1 mixes 2 and 5 decimal places; align decimal precision (e.g., 1.91 instead of 1.90686) for consistent presentation.
The graph effectively shows condition means, but error bars (SD or SE) should be added to visualise variability and support interpretation.
Interpretation of means and SDs is concise and directly linked to the hypothesis, demonstrating solid linkage between data and research question.
The rationale for using the one-tailed Mann-Whitney U test is valid, but the explanation âassuming the null hypothesis is trueâ may confuse readers; clarify decision criteria instead.
p-values and significance levels are inconsistently formatted (e.g., âp < 0.05â vs â0.4564â); adopt a uniform style (e.g., three decimal places) throughout.
The discussion briefly relates results to Huchendorfâs findings and theory, but critical engagement with why the effect may be absent would strengthen evaluation.
The conclusion restates non-significance but does not synthesise broader implications or future directions; refine it to emphasise study contribution.
The reference list includes URLs but lacks access dates, which are required for online sources in APA style.
Citation style for Laerd Statistics entry is inconsistent with journal article formatting; ensure uniform application of the chosen style guide.
